text: Graph Pattern: Graph representations. Adjacency list: dict/list of neighbors. Space O(V+E). Fast for sparse graphs. Adjacency matrix: 2D array, matrix[u][v]=weight. Space O(V²). Fast for dense graphs or edge lookup. Edge list: list of (u,v,w) tuples. Use for Kruskal's MST. Choose based on: density (sparse→list, dense→matrix), operations needed (neighbor iteration→list, edge lookup→matrix).

Graph Pattern: Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union). Supports: find(x) — root of x's component, union(x,y) — merge components. Optimizations: path compression (point directly to root) and union by rank/size. Time: nearly O(1) per operation amortized (inverse Ackermann). Use for: connected components, cycle detection in undirected graph, Kruskal's MST, dynamic connectivity.

Graph Pattern: Minimum spanning tree — Kruskal's. Sort edges by weight. Use Union-Find. Process edges in order: if endpoints in different components, add edge to MST and union. Stop when n-1 edges added. Time: O(E log E) for sort + O(E α(V)) for union-find. Space: O(V). For dense graphs, Prim's O(E log V) with heap may be faster.

Graph Pattern: Minimum spanning tree — Prim's. Start from any node. Maintain min-heap of (weight, node, parent). Greedily pick minimum weight edge connecting MST to non-MST node. Time: O(E log V) with heap. Space: O(V). Use for dense graphs (heap on vertices) or when adjacency matrix given. Both Kruskal and Prim produce correct MST; choice is implementation preference.

Graph Pattern: Bellman-Ford shortest path. Relax all edges V-1 times. dp[v] = min distance to v. For each edge (u,v,w): dp[v] = min(dp[v], dp[u]+w). After V-1 rounds, check again: if any edge can still be relaxed, negative cycle exists. Time: O(VE), Space: O(V). Use when: graph has negative weights, need to detect negative cycles. Slower than Dijkstra for non-negative weights.

Graph Pattern: Floyd-Warshall all-pairs shortest path. dp[i][j] = shortest path from i to j. Initialize with edge weights (infinity if no edge). For each intermediate vertex k: dp[i][j] = min(dp[i][j], dp[i][k]+dp[k][j]). Time: O(V³), Space: O(V²). Use for: small dense graphs, all-pairs shortest paths, reachability matrix (use OR instead of min). Detects negative cycles if dp[i][i] < 0.

Graph Pattern: Topological sort — course schedule. Kahn's: compute in-degrees, BFS from 0-in-degree nodes. DFS: post-order traversal, push to stack after children done. Use for: build dependencies, package manager, task ordering. Cycle detection: if not all nodes processed in Kahn's, cycle exists. Time: O(V+E). Real use: make/cmake, npm, import ordering.

Graph Pattern: Bipartite graph detection. BFS/DFS coloring with 2 colors. Assign alternating colors. If same-color conflict: not bipartite. Use for: can problem be 2-coloured (e.g., divide into two groups with no internal conflicts), matching problems. Bipartite graphs have no odd-length cycles. Time: O(V+E). Maximum bipartite matching: Hungarian algorithm O(VE) or Hopcroft-Karp O(E√V).

Graph Pattern: Strongly connected components — Tarjan's. Single DFS tracking discovery time and low-link values. Low[v] = min discovery time reachable via tree edges + one back edge. Push nodes to stack during DFS. When low[v]==disc[v]: pop stack as SCC root. Time: O(V+E). Detects SCCs in directed graphs. Use for: strongly connected systems, 2-SAT, condensation graph.

Graph Pattern: Bridges and articulation points — Tarjan's variant. Bridge: edge (u,v) where low[v] > disc[u] (v can't reach u without this edge). Articulation point: root with 2+ children in DFS tree, or non-root u with child v where low[v] >= disc[u]. Track parent to avoid revisiting parent edge. Time: O(V+E). Use for: network reliability, critical connections in network.

Graph Pattern: Eulerian path and circuit. Eulerian circuit: every vertex has even degree (undirected) or in-degree==out-degree (directed). Eulerian path: exactly 0 or 2 vertices with odd degree (undirected). Hierholzer's algorithm: start from valid node, greedily traverse edges, push to result when no more edges from current node. Time: O(V+E). Use for: Chinese postman, circuit reconstruction.

Graph Pattern: Network flow — Ford-Fulkerson / Edmonds-Karp. Max flow from source to sink. Augment along paths from source to sink. BFS (Edmonds-Karp) finds shortest augmenting path. Update residual graph: reduce forward edge, increase backward edge. Time: O(VE²) Edmonds-Karp. Max flow = Min cut (max-flow min-cut theorem). Use for: bipartite matching, scheduling, circulation problems.

Graph Pattern: Shortest path with states — modified Dijkstra. Standard Dijkstra on augmented state space. State: (node, additional_info). Example: shortest path with at most k stops → state (node, stops_used). Cheapest flights: dp[k][v] = min cost reaching v with at most k stops. Time: O(k*E*log(k*V)). Also: shortest path with fuel, path collecting keys.

Graph Pattern: Grid as graph. Each cell is a node. 4-directional edges (up, down, left, right) or 8-directional. Edge weights: uniform (BFS), varied (Dijkstra), 0/1 (0-1 BFS). State: (row, col) or augmented (row, col, extra_state). Use for: shortest path in maze, flood fill, number of islands. Time: O(m*n) for BFS/DFS, O(m*n*log(m*n)) for Dijkstra on grid.

Graph Pattern: Cycle detection — directed graph. DFS with 3-color marking: WHITE (unvisited), GRAY (in current DFS path/stack), BLACK (fully processed). If we reach GRAY node: back edge → cycle. For undirected: skip parent during DFS, if visited non-parent neighbor found → cycle. Union-Find for undirected: if both endpoints in same component → cycle. Time: O(V+E).

Graph Pattern: Shortest path — A* algorithm. Like Dijkstra but with heuristic. Priority = actual_dist + heuristic(node, goal). Heuristic must be admissible (never overestimates). Common heuristic for grid: Manhattan distance, Euclidean distance. Faster than Dijkstra when good heuristic available. Optimal when heuristic is admissible. Time: depends on heuristic quality, O((V+E) log V) worst case.

Graph Pattern: 2-SAT. Given clauses of form (x OR y), determine if satisfying assignment exists. Build implication graph: (NOT x → y) and (NOT y → x) for each clause. Find SCCs. If any variable x and NOT x are in same SCC: unsatisfiable. Otherwise: variable value determined by SCC topological order. Time: O(V+E). Use for: constraint satisfaction problems with binary clauses.

Graph Pattern: Minimum vertex cover / maximum matching. König's theorem: in bipartite graphs, minimum vertex cover = maximum matching size. Find max matching (Hopcroft-Karp), then use alternating paths from unmatched left nodes to find vertex cover. Time: O(E√V). Use for: minimum guards to cover all edges, job assignment, scheduling conflicts.

Graph Pattern: Traveling salesman problem (TSP). Find shortest Hamiltonian cycle. Exact: bitmask DP, dp[mask][v] = min cost visiting nodes in mask ending at v. Transition: try adding unvisited node. Time: O(2^n * n²), Space: O(2^n * n). Approximations: nearest neighbor (greedy), 2-opt improvement, Christofides algorithm (1.5 approximation). NP-hard in general.

Graph Pattern: Shortest path on DAG. DAGs have no cycles, allowing O(V+E) shortest path via topological order. Process nodes in topo order; for each node, relax outgoing edges. Handles negative edges (unlike Dijkstra). Longest path: negate weights or change relaxation to max. Use for: critical path in project scheduling (CPM), longest increasing subsequence as DAG.
